human:
POINTS:
- [rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java:184] Thread creation is fast and we're going through an idle timeout on client side anyway before the thread on server is released. So keeping this at 1m for simplicity.
v5:
SUMMARY:
This PR replaces unbounded `Executors.newCachedThreadPool()` in LocalDataTransferServer with a bounded `ThreadPoolExecutor` and adds config support. The change is intentional and well-structured, but has a missing shutdown call, an unhandled rejection scenario, and a shared-default constant that affects BookKeeperServer too.

POINTS:
- [rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java:182] The `ExecutorService threadPool` is a local variable created inside `run()` and is never shut down when the server stops. When `stop()` closes the listener, the `while(true)` loop exits via the `IOException` handler, but `threadPool.shutdown()` or `shutdownNow()` is never called. This leaves worker threads alive until their 60s keep-alive expires, causing thread leaks on restart. Move the executor to a field and add a `shutdown()` call in `stop()`.

- [rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java:182-186] The `ThreadPoolExecutor` is constructed with a `SynchronousQueue` and maxThreads=4096, using the default `AbortPolicy`. When 4096 concurrent clients are connected and a new `execute()` call occurs, `RejectedExecutionException` (a `RuntimeException`) will be thrown — but it is not caught in the `while` loop (which catches only `AsynchronousCloseException` and `IOException`). The accept loop will crash. Consider adding a custom `RejectedExecutionHandler` (e.g., `CallerRunsPolicy` or a handler that logs and retries the accept) or explicitly catching `RejectedExecutionException`.

- [rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CacheConfig.java:161] Changing `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` from `Integer.MAX_VALUE` to `4096` affects **both** `getServerMaxThreads()` and the new `getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads()`. Our investigation confirmed that `getServerMaxThreads()` is used by `BookKeeperServer` (line 141 of `BookKeeperServer.java`) to set Thrift's `TThreadPoolServer.maxWorkerThreads()`. This means existing BookKeeperServer deployments that do not explicitly configure `rubix.cache.bookkeeper.max-threads` will silently change from essentially unbounded to a 4096 thread cap. The PR title says "Use bounded thread pools for LocalDataTransferServer **and** BookKeeperServer" — if this is intentional, add a changelog note documenting this behavioral change so operators are aware.

- [rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CacheConfig.java:332-335] The new `getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads()` correctly has its own config key (`rubix.local.transfer.max-threads`) so operators can tune the two servers independently. Well done on this separation.

- [rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CommonUtilities.java:88-94] The `threadsNamed()` helper is a clean, reusable addition. Minor note: the Javadoc says "a string will be unique to this instance… and will be assigned sequentially" — verify that `ThreadFactoryBuilder` actually appends a sequential counter (it does by default — this is correct, just noting it's worth confirming in the Guava version used by this project).
v6:
SUMMARY:
PR replaces unbounded `Executors.newCachedThreadPool()` in LocalDataTransferServer with a bounded `ThreadPoolExecutor` (max 4096 threads) and changes the shared `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` from `Integer.MAX_VALUE` to `4096`, which also affects BookKeeperServer's Thrift `TThreadPoolServer`.

POINTS:
- [rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java:53] Unused import `getServerMaxThreads` is brought in but never referenced in the changed code — only `getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads` is used. Remove this import to avoid confusion.
- [rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java:179] The `ExecutorService threadPool` is declared as a local variable in `run()` and is never shut down. When `stopServer()` → `localServer.stop()` closes the listener socket, the `run()` loop exits via `AsynchronousCloseException`, but `threadPool.shutdown()` is never called. While the 60-second keep-alive on the new bounded pool means idle threads will eventually terminate, this is still a resource-leak pattern. Consider promoting `threadPool` to a class field and adding `threadPool.shutdownNow()` (followed by `awaitTermination`) in `LocalServer.stop()`. Other components in the codebase (e.g., `ObjectPool.java:110-122`, `ObjectPoolPartition.java:225-236`) follow explicit shutdown conventions.
- [rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CacheConfig.java:161] Changing `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` from `Integer.MAX_VALUE` to `4096` affects both the new `getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads()` and the existing `getServerMaxThreads()`, which is called by `BookKeeperServer.java:141` to set `TThreadPoolServer.maxWorkerThreads()`. This is consistent with the PR's goal, but note that 4096 is the *default* — users who rely on the old unbounded behavior would need to explicitly set `rubix.cache.bookkeeper.max-threads` to a larger value to restore it. A changelog or release note mentioning this default change would be helpful.
- [rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CacheConfig.java:332] The new `getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads()` falls back to `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` (4096), which means both the local transfer server and BookKeeper server share the same default. This seems intentional, but consider whether the local transfer server should have its own dedicated default (e.g., a smaller value if its workload is lighter) rather than reusing the BookKeeper default.
- [rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CommonUtilities.java:87] The new `threadsNamed()` helper is a clean utility. One minor suggestion: the Javadoc mentions "a string will be unique to this instance of the ThreadFactory and will be assigned sequentially" — consider clarifying that the sequential counter is managed internally by Guava's `ThreadFactoryBuilder`, so callers just need to supply the format string (e.g., `"lds-worker-%s"`).
